messages.content: script about the new conference with oklahoma city pan american northeast louisiana hardin simmons cenetary samford and mercer in 1978

Narrator: It’s 1978, and there’s a new conference on the block. The Oklahoma City, Pan American, Northeast Louisiana, Hardin Simmons, Centenary, Samford, and Mercer universities have joined forces to create a new conference.

(Scene opens with representatives from each university sitting around a table)

Representative from Oklahoma City: I’m excited to be a part of this new conference. It’s going to give us more competition and exposure.

Representative from Pan American: I agree. It’s always good to play against teams that have different styles of play.

Representative from Northeast Louisiana: Plus, it gives us a chance to expand our recruiting pool.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: And we can play schools that are closer to our region.

Representative from Centenary: Exactly. We don’t have to travel as far, which will save us money on travel expenses.

Representative from Samford: And we get to play against some great teams.

Representative from Mercer: It’s a win-win for everyone involved.

Narrator: The new conference will be called the Gulf Star Conference and will provide opportunities for each university to compete in their respective sports.

Representative from Oklahoma City: Since we’re all from the South, we can relate to each other and our styles of play. This conference is going to be tough.

Representative from Pan American: But I’m confident that we’ll all rise to the occasion and give it our all.

Representative from Northeast Louisiana: I’m looking forward to playing against some of the best teams in the region.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: Yeah, it’s going to be tough, but it’s going to be worth it.

Representative from Centenary: Who knows, maybe we’ll even get some national recognition.

Representative from Samford: Whatever happens, we’ll stick together and make this conference a success.

Representative from Mercer: I couldn’t agree more. We’re all in this together.

Narrator: And with that, the representatives from the Gulf Star Conference universities shake hands and prepare for the upcoming season, knowing that they have a tough road ahead of them but are ready to face the challenges head-on.
after year 1 oklahoma city and pan american left for the midwestern city conference (now horizon league) and to go independent respectively

Narrator: After year one of the Gulf Star Conference, some changes were already happening. Oklahoma City and Pan American universities had decided to leave the conference for different reasons.

Representative from Oklahoma City: We’ve realized that we want to compete at a higher level and we feel like joining the Midwestern City Conference (now known as the Horizon League) will give us that opportunity.

Representative from Pan American: We want to try going independent for a year to see how it goes. It’s tough being in a conference and we want to see if we can handle scheduling our own games.

Narrator: Although these departures were unexpected, the remaining universities continued to push forward with the Gulf Star Conference.

Representative from Northeast Louisiana: Losing Oklahoma City and Pan American is tough, but it doesn't mean that we can't continue to have a successful conference.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: We’ll have to work harder to promote ourselves and attract new quality members to the conference.

Representative from Centenary: We’re all still committed to making the Gulf Star Conference a success.

Representative from Samford: It may not be an easy road, but we’re ready to face the challenges.

Representative from Mercer: Our conference may be smaller now, but we can still make a name for ourselves.

Narrator: And so, the Gulf Star Conference continued on with more determination than ever. Despite losing two universities, the remaining members knew that their priorities were to work hard, promote themselves, and attract new members to make the conference stronger than ever before.
after losing oklahoma city and pan american, the gulf star is back up to 8 with the addition of northwestern state and little rock

Narrator: After the departure of Oklahoma City and Pan American, the remaining members of the Gulf Star Conference continued to push forward. And it wasn't long until they found new members to replace those who left.

Representative from Northwestern State: Joining the Gulf Star Conference is a great opportunity for us. We're excited to become a part of this new conference and compete against some great programs.

Representative from Little Rock: We're joining the Gulf Star Conference and we see this as a chance to build ourselves and help grow the conference.

Representative from Northeast Louisiana: It's great to see the Gulf Star Conference grow stronger with new members.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: It's great to have more members and new teams to play against.

Representative from Centenary: We're looking forward to building new rivalries with these two new universities.

Representative from Samford: I'm excited about the possibilities of playing teams we may not have played before.

Representative from Mercer: It's really encouraging to see the Gulf Star Conference expand so quickly.

Narrator: With Northwestern State and Little Rock now a part of the conference, the Gulf Star Conference was back up to eight teams. Now with a bigger field of competitors, the Gulf Star Conference had its sights set on becoming a competitive, successful and respected conference.
(1980) the gulf star expands up to 9 with the addition of georgia southern

Narrator: Two years after the departure of Oklahoma City and Pan American and the addition of Northwestern State and Little Rock, the Gulf Star Conference continued to grow stronger. In 1980, they found another university to join their ranks.

Representative from Georgia Southern: We're excited to be joining the Gulf Star Conference. We believe it's going to be a great opportunity for our university as well as for the conference.

Representative from Northeast Louisiana: We couldn't agree more. Having more members in our conference will bring more opportunities for us to compete and play against some great teams.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: Our conference is expanding and we're becoming stronger. Each new addition is making us a more competitive and respected conference.

Representative from Centenary: With Georgia Southern joining us, we have nine universities now, and that means more competition and more chances to show our talents.

Representative from Samford: We're looking forward to playing against Georgia Southern and seeing how we match up against their team.

Representative from Mercer: Expanding our conference will create more excitement among our universities. The Gulf Star Conference is growing stronger with each new member.

Narrator: And so, with the arrival of Georgia Southern, the Gulf Star Conference now had nine universities. The conference was expanding, and becoming stronger. Every university was determined to create a conference that would be highly competitive, and respected throughout the region. The future looked bright for the Gulf Star Conference.
its now 1982, and northeast louisiana is leaving for the southland. the gulf star replaces nlu with nicholls state who is a d2 call up

Narrator: Two years after the arrival of Georgia Southern, the Gulf Star Conference was dealt another blow. Northeast Louisiana had decided to leave the Gulf Star Conference for the Southland Conference.

Representative from Northeast Louisiana: While we have enjoyed our time in the Gulf Star Conference, we feel that the Southland Conference will provide us with more stability and opportunities.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: We’re sorry to see them go, but we wish them all the best of luck in the Southland Conference.

Representative from Centenary: This is an opportunity for the Gulf Star Conference to find a new university and continue to expand and grow.

Representative from Samford: We’re ready to face the challenges ahead and move forward.

Representative from Mercer: We need to continue to work hard and promote the Gulf Star Conference to attract more universities.

Narrator: And so, with the departure of Northeast Louisiana, the Gulf Star Conference was once again searching for a new university to replace them. This time, they found a Division II call-up in Nicholls State.

Representative from Nicholls State: We’re excited to be moving up to Division I and joining the Gulf Star Conference. This is a great opportunity for our university.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: We’re happy to welcome Nicholls State to the Gulf Star Conference. It’s great to keep our conference growing.

Representative from Centenary: Having Nicholls State join our conference keeps the competition level high and keeps the conference fresh.

Representative from Samford: It will be great to play against Nicholls State and see what they can bring to the table.

Representative from Mercer: The Gulf Star Conference may have lost a member, but we’ve gained a new one that brings something different to the conference.

Narrator: And so, with the addition of Nicholls State, the Gulf Star Conference continued its growth. The conference may have lost Northeast Louisiana, but the remaining universities were determined to keep pushing forward and making the Gulf Star Conference a competitive and respected conference.
in 1983 , the gulf star is up to 10 with the addition of georgia state

Narrator: The Gulf Star Conference continued to expand, and one year after the addition of Nicholls State, a new university joined the conference: Georgia State University.

Representative from Georgia State: We’re thrilled to be joining the Gulf Star Conference. We see this as a great opportunity for our university to compete at the Division I level.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: We’re excited to welcome Georgia State to the Gulf Star Conference. Having more members will make us a stronger and more competitive conference.

Representative from Centenary: The competition level just got even higher with the addition of Georgia State.

Representative from Samford: We’re excited to play against a new school and see what they can bring to the conference.

Representative from Mercer: The Gulf Star Conference continues to grow and we’re happy to be a part of it.

Narrator: With Georgia State joining the conference, the Gulf Star Conference was now up to ten universities. Each university was excited to play against one another and continue to push themselves to be better. The conference had come a long way from its humble beginnings, and there was no doubt that the Gulf Star Conference was there to stay.
1984 nicholls state and northwestern state would leave to form a new conference with 4 other schools in texas and louisiana

Narrator: Just one year after the arrival of Georgia State, the Gulf Star Conference was dealt another surprise. Nicholls State and Northwestern State had decided to leave the Gulf Star Conference and form a new conference with four other schools in Texas and Louisiana.

Representative from Nicholls State: We’ve decided to leave the Gulf Star Conference in order to form a new conference with schools that are closer to our region.

Representative from Northwestern State: It’s a tough decision to leave a conference, especially one that has been so great to us. But we believe that this new conference will better serve us.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: We’re sad to see them go but we wish them all the best in their new venture.

Representative from Centenary: The Gulf Star Conference will continue to move forward and continue to be a respected Division I conference.

Representative from Samford: As a conference, we will continue to work hard and remain competitive. 

Representative from Mercer: We’ll have to work even harder to attract new universities to join us.

Narrator: Losing two universities was tough for the Gulf Star Conference, but they remained determined to keep pushing forward. The remaining universities knew that they would have to work even harder to make a name for themselves.

Despite this setback, the Gulf Star Conference continued to compete and remain a competitive Division 1 conference with the hopes that they will continue to attract more universities to join them in the future.
in 1985 the gulf star would go up to 9 with the addition of stetson

Narrator: A year after the departure of Nicholls State and Northwestern State, the Gulf Star Conference added a new member university - Stetson University.

Representative from Stetson: We’re excited to be joining the Gulf Star Conference and are looking forward to competing with some of the best schools in the region.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: We’re happy to welcome Stetson to the Gulf Star Conference. Our conference continues to grow and improve.

Representative from Centenary: With Stetson being a new member, it will be interesting to see how they stack up against the other schools.

Representative from Samford: Having another university in the conference creates new opportunities for us to play and compete with different teams.

Representative from Mercer: We’re excited to see the Gulf Star Conference grow and the addition of Stetson shows that we're still a respected Division I conference.

Narrator: With Stetson's addition, the Gulf Star Conference was up to nine universities. Despite the setbacks of losing Nicholls State and Northwestern State the previous year, the conference continued to push forward. They remained committed to building a respected Division I conference, and each new addition brought them one step closer to that goal.
the following school year 1986 utsa would join making the conference up to 10

Narrator: The Gulf Star Conference continued its expansion in the following school year. The University of Texas at San Antonio (UTSA) joined the conference as the 10th university.

Representative from UTSA: We’re thrilled to be joining the Gulf Star Conference. This is an excellent opportunity for our university to play and compete with other Division I programs.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: We’re excited to welcome UTSA to the Gulf Star Conference. Every new addition strengthens our conference.

Representative from Centenary: With UTSA joining us, the competition level just got even higher. We’re excited to see what they can bring to the table.

Representative from Samford: It’s always exciting to play against new schools. We can’t wait to face UTSA on the court.

Representative from Mercer: The Gulf Star Conference has come a long way, and the conference is growing stronger every year. 

Narrator: With UTSA’s addition, the Gulf Star Conference reached a new milestone, having its maximum 10 universities. The conference continued to showcase the top talent in the region and was becoming a recognized and respected force in Division I play. Each university was eager to compete with the best in the conference and show their worth. The Gulf Star Conference had come a long way from its early beginnings and has proven to be here to stay.
houston baptist would drop to naia in 1989

Narrator: In 1989, the Gulf Star Conference faced another setback when Houston Baptist University made the decision to drop to the NAIA level of competition.

Representative from Houston Baptist: We’re grateful for our time in the Gulf Star Conference, but we believe that the NAIA level of competition suits our university better.

Representative from Hardin Simmons: We’re disappointed to see Houston Baptist go, but we understand their reasons for making this decision.

Representative from Centenary: The Gulf Star Conference continues to push forward even when we face challenges like this.

Representative from Samford: It will be tough to lose Houston Baptist, but we’ll continue to work hard and strive to create a competitive and respected conference.

Representative from Mercer: No matter what, we remain committed to making the Gulf Star Conference a successful Division I conference.

Narrator: Despite the loss of Houston Baptist, the remaining universities were determined to keep pushing forward, to continue the growth of the conference and attract new universities to join in the future. The Gulf Star Conference would not let setbacks discourage them from their goal of becoming a renowned Division I conference.
in 1990 hardin simmons dropped to division 3 and fiu joined

Narrator: The Gulf Star Conference continued to evolve, and in 1990, it faced another big change. Hardin-Simmons University made the decision to drop down to Division III level of competition.

Representative from Hardin-Simmons: It’s a tough decision to leave the Gulf Star Conference, but we believe that the Division III level of competition suits our university better.

Representative from Centenary: We’re sad to see Hardin-Simmons go, but we wish them all the best in their new venture.

Representative from Samford: As a conference, we’ll continue to work hard and remain competitive.

Representative from Mercer: We’ll have to continue to attract new universities to join us and strengthen our conference.

Narrator: Despite the loss of Hardin-Simmons, the Gulf Star Conference added a new university: Florida International University (FIU).

Representative from FIU: We’re excited to join the Gulf Star Conference and compete against other Division I programs.

Representative from Centenary: We’re happy to welcome FIU to the Gulf Star Conference. Each new addition strengthens our conference.

Representative from Samford: With FIU as a new member, we’re excited to play against a new school and see what they can bring to the conference.

Representative from Mercer: The Gulf Star Conference continues to evolve and grow. With FIU joining us, the competition level just got even higher.

Narrator: With the addition of FIU, the Gulf Star Conference was once again a respected Division I conference, with nine universities remaining. Though they had faced some setbacks in the past, they continued to push forward, evolving and proving their strength as a respected conference in Division I sport.
little rock and utsa left for the sun belt and southland conferences respectively in 1991

Narrator: The Gulf Star Conference faced more changes in 1991 when two more universities decided to leave. The University of Arkansas at Little Rock (Little Rock) and the University of Texas at San Antonio (UTSA) both decided to leave the conference.

Representative from Little Rock: We’ve had a great time in the Gulf Star Conference, but we believe that moving to the Sun Belt Conference will better suit our university’s program.

Representative from UTSA: Our program has grown and developed over the years, and we believe that moving to the Southland Conference is the next step for our university.

Representative from Centenary: We’re sad to see Little Rock and UTSA leave, but we wish them all the best in their new venture.

Representative from Samford: As a conference, we’ll continue to work hard and remain competitive. 

Representative from Mercer: It’s a great opportunity for the Gulf Star Conference to attract new universities and strengthen our conference.

Narrator: Although the Gulf Star Conference lost two more universities, they remained determined to keep pushing forward and building a respected Division I conference. They continued to examine and evaluate their strengths and weaknesses in order to attract new members. The conference knew that in a rapidly changing and incredibly competitive Division I landscape, the future success of their conference would depend on their ability to evolve and adapt to the changes.
sela and cofc were added to replace little rock and utsa

Narrator: After the departure of Little Rock and UTSA in 1991, the Gulf Star Conference was once again searching for new members. In 1992, they found two new universities to replace them: Southeastern Louisiana University (SELA) and the College of Charleston (C
